name: ECB / SSM & internal models
description: How euro-area banks are supervised and how their internal models get reviewed on-site — the work of acting for the ECB to check a bank's risk management.
primerTitle: ECB / SSM & internal models in one page

glossary.ECB: European Central Bank — the central bank of the eurozone and the lead supervisor under the Single Supervisory Mechanism
glossary.SSM: Single Supervisory Mechanism — the European banking supervision system placing significant eurozone banks under ECB direct supervision
glossary.SREP: Supervisory Review and Evaluation Process — the annual supervisory assessment of a bank's risks, governance, capital and liquidity
glossary.P2R: Pillar 2 Requirement — a binding, institution-specific capital add-on set by the supervisor under SREP
glossary.P2G: Pillar 2 Guidance — a non-binding, institution-specific capital expectation set above P2R
glossary.IRB: Internal Ratings-Based approach — the Basel credit-risk framework allowing approved banks to use their own PD, LGD and EAD estimates
glossary.JST: Joint Supervisory Team — the mixed ECB/NCA team that directly supervises a Significant Institution under the SSM
glossary.NCA: National Competent Authority — the national banking supervisor that participates in SSM supervision alongside the ECB
glossary.RWA: Risk-Weighted Assets — exposures scaled by regulatory risk weights, the denominator of capital ratios
glossary.PD: Probability of Default — the estimated likelihood a borrower defaults over a stated horizon
glossary.LGD: Loss Given Default — the fraction of exposure lost if default occurs, after recoveries
glossary.EAD: Exposure at Default — the estimated amount owed at the time of default
glossary.TRIM: Targeted Review of Internal Models — an ECB supervisory exercise assessing banks' internal model compliance with CRR requirements
glossary.EBA: European Banking Authority — the EU body issuing binding regulatory technical standards and guidelines
glossary.CRR: Capital Requirements Regulation — the EU regulation directly transposing Basel capital rules

primer:
**SSM architecture.** The **Single Supervisory Mechanism (SSM)**, live since November 2014, is the euro-area system in which the **ECB** and **national competent authorities (NCAs)** jointly supervise banks: the ECB directly supervises **significant institutions (SIs)**, NCAs handle **less-significant ones (LSIs)** under ECB oversight. A bank is **significant** if it meets criteria such as assets > **EUR 30bn**, > **20% of national GDP**, or being among the three largest in its country (~110 SI groups hold most euro-area assets). A **Joint Supervisory Team (JST)** — ECB + NCA staff led by an ECB coordinator — runs day-to-day supervision: planning inspections, the SREP and follow-up. The **SREP** (Supervisory Review & Evaluation Process) is the annual cycle scoring business model, governance, capital and liquidity risks, then setting bank-specific requirements (the counterpart to the bank's **ICAAP/ILAAP**). Its outputs: **P2R** (Pillar 2 Requirement — *binding* capital add-on) and **P2G** (Pillar 2 Guidance — *non-binding* buffer expectation); overall requirement = Pillar 1 + P2R + combined buffers (+ P2G). The ECB sets multi-year **supervisory priorities** (credit risk/provisioning, internal-model/model-risk deficiencies, IT/cyber & operational resilience, climate & environmental risk).

**On-site & investigations.** An **on-site inspection (OSI)** is an in-depth, time-bound investigation at the bank's premises into a specific risk/process/control, run by a head-of-mission and team — distinct from off-site monitoring. An **internal-model investigation (IMI)** is a specialised on-site review of whether a model may be approved, extended, changed or stays compliant, combining regulatory assessment with quantitative testing of data, methodology and performance. **TRIM** (Targeted Review of Internal Models, 2016–2021) reviewed ~200 models across SIs to confirm compliance and cut unwarranted **RWA variability**, seeding the ECB Guide to Internal Models. **Findings** are graded by severity (commonly **F1 low → F4 very high**), each tied to remediation deadlines the JST tracks. **IMAS** is the SSM's workflow system (with a bank-facing portal for model-change requests/applications). The **external specialist** typically replicates model outputs on the bank's data, tests data quality/representativeness, builds **challenger/benchmark** models, reviews methodology and governance, and drafts evidence for findings.

**Internal models & validation.** With supervisory permission a bank may use **internal models** instead of standardised approaches for own-funds: **IRB** (credit), **IMM** (counterparty), **IMA** (market). A **risk-parameter review** scrutinises how **PD/LGD/EAD** are *estimated* (data history, default sample, segmentation, calibration, conservatism); expected loss $\mathrm{EL}=\mathrm{PD}\times\mathrm{LGD}\times\mathrm{EAD}$. The bank's **internal validation function** must be *independent of development* and validate at least annually (discriminatory power, calibration, stability). The **use test** requires the model is actually used in day-to-day decisions (limits, pricing, provisioning), not kept only for capital. The **margin of conservatism (MoC)** is an explicit add-on for deficiencies — category **A** (data/methodology), **B** (estimation error), **C** (general caution). **Rating philosophy**: **point-in-time (PIT)** reflects current conditions (PDs rise in downturns); **through-the-cycle (TTC)** averages over the cycle (stable PDs) — the choice drives procyclicality and must match calibration. **Downturn LGD** is calibrated to recession conditions, since recoveries fall when defaults cluster. **Ongoing model monitoring (OMM)** tracks performance between validations (backtesting, stability, override rates), reported to the ECB on schedule. **Model risk management (MRM)** is the lifecycle governance discipline (inventory, development, validation, approval, change control, decommissioning).

**Rules & guides.** The **ECB Guide to Internal Models (EGIM)** is the consolidated statement of supervisory expectations (general + credit/market/counterparty chapters; ~2019 out of TRIM, revised 2024–2025) — the reference an inspector measures a model against. **CRR/CRD** are the EU's Basel transposition: the **Capital Requirements Regulation** (directly applicable rules, IRB permission conditions) and **Directive** (national law, supervisory powers, Pillar 2) — Basel is the standard, CRR/CRD the binding law. **Supervisory benchmarking** (Art. 78 CRD) is an annual EBA/ECB exercise running banks' models on common portfolios to flag outliers. The **definition of default** is harmonised by EBA: **90 days past due** on a material exposure, or judged unlikely to pay. The **EBA IRB repair programme** re-harmonised IRB (PD/LGD, downturn LGD, default definition) after TRIM, narrowing discretion behind RWA variability. The **output floor** (Basel III / CRR3) backstops model-based RWA at **≥ 72.5%** of the standardised computation, capping internal-model capital benefit. **Climate & environmental (C&E) risk** is a stated priority — banks must embed it into governance, risk management and, increasingly, models and stress tests.

cards.definition: The euro-area banking-supervision system, live since November 2014, in which the [[ECB]] and national competent authorities ([[NCA]]s) jointly supervise banks. The [[ECB]] directly supervises the significant institutions; [[NCA]]s handle the rest under [[ECB]] oversight.
cards.note: The 'who supervises whom' backbone of everything else here.

cards.definition: A bank is significant — and supervised directly by the [[ECB]] — if it meets criteria such as assets above EUR 30bn, more than 20% of national GDP, or being among the three largest in its country. Others are less-significant, supervised by the [[NCA]].
cards.note: ~110 significant groups hold the bulk of euro-area banking assets.

cards.definition: The team that runs day-to-day supervision of each significant institution, staffed by [[ECB]] and [[NCA]] supervisors and led by an [[ECB]] coordinator. It plans inspections, runs the [[SREP]] and follows up on findings.
cards.note: The 'client team' an external specialist effectively supports.

cards.definition: The annual cycle in which supervisors score a bank's business model, governance, capital risks and liquidity risks, then set bank-specific requirements. The supervisory counterpart to the bank's own ICAAP/ILAAP.
cards.note: Output drives Pillar 2 add-ons (see [[P2R]] / [[P2G]]).

cards.definition: [[SREP]] outputs: the Pillar 2 Requirement is a binding, bank-specific capital add-on above Pillar 1 minimums; Pillar 2 Guidance is a non-binding expectation for an additional buffer. Breaching P2R is a hard breach; missing P2G triggers dialogue.
cards.formula: Overall capital requirement $=$ Pillar 1 $+$ P2R $+$ combined buffers $(+\,\text{P2G})$
cards.note: Binding vs non-binding is the distinction interviewers probe.

cards.definition: The [[ECB]] sets multi-year supervisory priorities that steer inspections and reviews — recurring themes include credit risk and provisioning, internal-model and model-risk deficiencies, IT/cyber and operational resilience, and climate & environmental risk.
cards.note: Knowing the current priorities shows you track the supervisor's agenda.

cards.definition: An in-depth, time-bound investigation conducted at the bank's premises into a specific risk area, process or control. A dedicated head-of-mission and team gather evidence, test, and issue graded findings — distinct from ongoing off-site monitoring.
cards.note: The 'visiting other banks for the [[ECB]]' part of the role.

cards.definition: A specialised on-site review assessing whether a bank's internal model may be approved, extended or changed, or remains compliant. IMIs combine regulatory assessment with quantitative testing of the model's data, methodology and performance.
cards.note: Where a quant who can re-run models is most useful.

cards.definition: A major [[ECB]] project (2016–2021) that reviewed around 200 internal models across significant institutions to confirm their regulatory compliance and reduce unwarranted variability in risk-weighted assets ([[RWA]]). It standardised supervisory expectations and seeded the [[ECB]] Guide to Internal Models.
cards.note: The landmark exercise that professionalised internal-model review.

cards.definition: Inspection conclusions are documented as findings graded by severity (commonly F1 low through F4 very high), each tied to remediation obligations and deadlines that the [[JST]] then tracks to closure.
cards.note: Severity grading + remediation tracking = the output of a mission.

cards.definition: The Information Management System for the [[SSM]] — the workflow system supervisors use for processes and findings — with a bank-facing IMAS portal for submitting model-change requests, applications and documentation.
cards.note: The plumbing through which investigations and follow-ups flow.

cards.definition: On an IMI/OSI a consultant typically replicates the bank's model outputs on its data, tests data quality and representativeness, builds challenger/benchmark models, reviews methodology and governance documentation, and drafts evidence for findings.
cards.note: This is the day job — and it is your QA/validation skillset retargeted.

cards.definition: With supervisory permission a bank may use its own models instead of standardised approaches to compute own-funds requirements: [[IRB]] for credit risk, IMM for counterparty credit, and the internal models approach for market risk. More risk-sensitive, but model-dependent.
cards.note: Permission, ongoing compliance and change control are all supervised.

cards.definition: Reviewing an [[IRB]] model means scrutinising how its [[PD]], [[LGD]] and [[EAD]] parameters are estimated: data history and representativeness, default sample, segmentation, calibration, and the conservatism applied. (The parameters themselves are defined in the credit-risk deck.)
cards.note: Inspectors challenge the estimation, not just the formula.

cards.definition: The bank's own independent unit that validates models at least annually — separate from model development — checking discriminatory power, calibration and stability. Supervisors assess whether this function is genuinely independent and effective.
cards.note: Independence of validation from development is a recurring finding.

cards.definition: The requirement that an internal model is actually used in day-to-day risk management and decisions (limits, pricing, provisioning), not maintained solely to compute regulatory capital. A model that fails the use test is hard to approve.
cards.note: Regulators distrust 'capital-only' models.

cards.definition: An explicit add-on to risk-parameter estimates compensating for identified deficiencies — data/methodology limitations (category A), estimation error (B), and general caution (C). It quantifies uncertainty rather than hiding it.
cards.note: A direct analogue to systematic-uncertainty budgeting in physics.

cards.definition: A point-in-time rating reflects current conditions, so [[PD]]s rise in downturns; a through-the-cycle rating averages over the cycle, giving more stable [[PD]]s. The philosophy drives procyclicality and must match how the model is calibrated and used.
cards.note: Mixing philosophies between estimation and use is a classic flaw.

cards.definition: Loss-given-default calibrated to economic-downturn conditions, since recoveries fall when defaults cluster. [[EBA]] guidelines prescribe how to identify the downturn period and quantify the adjustment when data is scarce.
cards.note: Where 'use the long-run average' is not conservative enough.

cards.definition: Continuous tracking of a model's performance between full validations — backtesting outcomes, stability and override rates — so deterioration is caught early and reported. Banks report validation results to the [[ECB]] on a defined schedule.
cards.note: Monitoring + validation reporting = the model's health check.

cards.definition: The governance discipline covering a model's whole lifecycle — inventory, development standards, validation, approval, change control and decommissioning — to manage the risk of using wrong or misused models. A standing supervisory priority.
cards.note: The umbrella the validation and review work sits under.

cards.definition: The [[ECB]]'s consolidated statement of supervisory expectations for internal models — a general chapter plus credit-, market- and counterparty-risk chapters. First issued around 2019 out of [[TRIM]] and revised in 2024–2025.
cards.note: The reference an inspector measures a model against.

cards.definition: The EU's transposition of Basel: the Capital Requirements Regulation (directly applicable rules, including [[IRB]] permission conditions) and the Capital Requirements Directive (national law, supervisory powers, Pillar 2). Supplemented by [[EBA]] regulatory technical standards and guidelines. The legal basis for model approval.
cards.note: Basel is the standard; CRR/CRD is the binding EU law.

cards.definition: An annual [[EBA]]/[[ECB]] exercise in which banks run their internal models on common benchmark portfolios so supervisors can compare risk-weight outcomes and flag institutions whose estimates are outliers. A cross-bank check on model variability.
cards.note: The data-driven complement to on-site investigation.

cards.definition: Harmonised [[EBA]] trigger: a default is recognised at 90 days past due on a material exposure, or when the obligor is judged unlikely to pay. Materiality thresholds and the return-to-non-default rules are prescribed to align banks.
cards.note: Get the default flag wrong and every downstream [[PD]]/[[LGD]] is off.

cards.definition: A set of [[EBA]] guidelines and technical standards (on [[PD]]/[[LGD]] estimation, downturn [[LGD]] and the default definition) that re-harmonised the [[IRB]] framework after [[TRIM]], narrowing the discretion that drove [[RWA]] variability across banks.
cards.note: Why post-2021 [[IRB]] models look more alike than they used to.

cards.definition: A Basel III / CRR3 backstop: model-based [[RWA]] cannot fall below 72.5% of the standardised computation, capping how much capital internal models can save and limiting cross-bank variability. (Mechanics in the regulation deck.)
cards.note: The ceiling on internal-model benefit — a live supervisory theme.

cards.definition: Supervisors now expect banks to embed climate and environmental risks into governance, risk management and, increasingly, models and stress tests. A relatively new front in inspections and a stated [[ECB]] priority.
cards.note: A growth area where a fast learner can add value early.
